articles.headline: Nationwide Trade Union Protests Against New Labour Codes Intensify
articles.explainer:
• Ten major trade unions orchestrated a nationwide protest against the new labour codes.
• Demands include the withdrawal of the new labour codes and stronger protection of workers' rights.
• Concerns raised over the dilution of worker protections, ease of hire and fire, and reduced collective bargaining power.
• The government reiterates that reforms aim to improve ease of doing business and formalize the labour sector.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Social empowerment
prelimsPoints: The four new labour codes are: Code on Wages, Industrial Relations Code, Code on Social Security, and Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code.
These codes aim to consolidate 29 central labour laws.
Key provisions causing concern include higher thresholds for retrenchment and changes in strike notice periods.
mainsPoints: Arguments for reforms: Ease of doing business, attracting investment, formalizing the economy, creating employment.
Arguments against reforms: Dilution of worker rights, increased precarity, weakening of trade unions, potential for exploitation, impact on social security.
Impact on gig economy: Discussion on how the new codes address or fail to address gig workers.
Way forward: Need for a balanced approach, social dialogue, and ensuring minimum wage and social security for all.
articles.imageDescription: Workers holding placards and banners during a nationwide protest against labour codes.
articles.trivia: The first comprehensive labour law in India was the Factories Act of 1881.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Federal Principles in Inter-State Water Dispute Verdict
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a landmark judgment clarifying the Centre-State roles in inter-state water resource management.
• The verdict emphasized the constitutional scheme of federalism and cooperative federalism in resolving disputes.
• It is expected to set a precedent for future inter-state resource allocation issues across the country.
• The judgment highlighted the role of tribunals and the Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction in such matters.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: Article 262 of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to legislate on inter-state river disputes.
The Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, provides for the adjudication of such disputes.
Water is a State subject (Entry 17, State List), but Parliament has powers for regulation and development of inter-state rivers (Entry 56, Union List).
mainsPoints: Challenges in water governance: Climate change impact, increasing demand, uneven distribution, political complexities.
Role of cooperative federalism: How states and centre can collaborate for sustainable water management.
Significance of the verdict: Strengthening federal structure, ensuring equitable resource distribution, reducing inter-state conflicts.
Recommendations: Need for national water policy, basin-level management, and enhanced data sharing.
articles.imageDescription: Gavel and Constitution of India symbolizing legal judgment.
articles.trivia: India has over 20 major inter-state river basins.

articles.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Exceeds Expectations, Driven by Manufacturing and Services
articles.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) released the Q2 (July-September) GDP figures for FY 2025-26.
• Growth rate surpassed analyst predictions, primarily fueled by robust performance in manufacturing and services sectors.
• Private consumption and investment also showed positive trends, indicating a broad-based economic recovery.
• Challenges remain in managing inflation and navigating global economic uncertainties, despite the strong performance.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Investment models
prelimsPoints: GDP is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
NSO, under the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI), is responsible for releasing GDP data.
The components of GDP typically include Consumption (C), Investment (I), Government Spending (G), and Net Exports (NX).
mainsPoints: Drivers of growth: Government capital expenditure, Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes, digital transformation, and services exports.
Sectoral analysis: Performance and contribution of agriculture, industry, and services sectors to overall growth.
Challenges: Global slowdown, supply chain disruptions, unemployment, income inequality, and inflation management.
Policy implications: Fiscal policy, monetary policy, and structural reforms needed for sustained and inclusive growth.
articles.imageDescription: A graphical representation of economic growth with upward arrows.
articles.trivia: India is currently the 5th largest economy in the world by nominal GDP.

articles.headline: India and France Strengthen Strategic Partnership in Annual Dialogue, Focus on Defence and Space
articles.explainer:
• The annual India-France Strategic Dialogue concluded, reaffirming the deep bilateral ties between the two nations.
• Key discussions revolved around enhancing defense cooperation, including co-development and co-production of military hardware.
• Space collaboration, particularly in satellite technology and space situational awareness, was a prominent agenda item.
• Both nations expressed a strong commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India and France elevated their relationship to a 'Strategic Partnership' in 1998.
Key defense deals between the two nations include the procurement of Rafale jets and Scorpene submarines.
ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) and CNES (French Space Agency) have a long-standing collaboration.
France is a permanent member of the UN Security Council.
mainsPoints: Pillars of cooperation: Defence, space, nuclear energy, counter-terrorism, and climate change.
Significance for India: Access to advanced technology, diversification of defense suppliers, strategic autonomy, and balancing China's influence in the Indo-Pacific.
Challenges: Geopolitical shifts, economic competition, and ensuring timely project delivery.
Future outlook: Deepening cooperation in emerging technologies and maritime security.
articles.imageDescription: Indian and French flags side-by-side, symbolizing diplomatic ties.
articles.trivia: France was the first country to establish a strategic partnership with India.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Disaster Management
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new-generation Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite is equipped with high-resolution imaging capabilities crucial for disaster management, urban planning, and agricultural monitoring.
• It will provide real-time data for tracking weather patterns, forest fires, and flood mapping.
• This launch further strengthens India's indigenous capabilities in space-based remote sensing and data collection.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency, headquartered in Bengaluru.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) and GSLV (Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle) are ISRO's primary launch vehicles.
Earth observation satellites are used for monitoring Earth's physical, chemical, and biological systems.
Sriharikota is home to the Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC), India's primary spaceport.
mainsPoints: Applications: Agriculture (crop health, yield estimation), urban planning, water resource management, climate studies, and border surveillance.
Contribution to disaster management: Early warning systems, damage assessment, and relief coordination.
Self-reliance in space: Reducing dependence on foreign satellites and boosting the domestic space industry.
Challenges: Space debris, cybersecurity of space assets, and international competition in the space sector.
articles.imageDescription: A rocket launching into space from a launchpad.
articles.trivia: India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched in 1975.

articles.headline: India Proposes New Global Climate Change Adaptation Fund at International Forum
articles.explainer:
• India, at a major international climate forum, advocated for the creation of a dedicated global fund for climate change adaptation.
• The proposal highlighted the disproportionate impact of climate change on developing nations and their limited resources for adaptation measures.
• Emphasized the need for equitable burden-sharing and technology transfer from developed to developing countries.
• The fund aims to support projects in areas like resilient infrastructure, sustainable agriculture, and early warning systems.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: The Adaptation Fund was established under the Kyoto Protocol.
The Green Climate Fund (GCF) is another major fund under the UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change).
Adaptation refers to adjustments in ecological, social, or economic systems in response to actual or expected climatic stimuli and their effects.
mainsPoints: Importance of adaptation: Reducing vulnerability, protecting livelihoods, and ensuring food security in the face of climate change.
Challenges in climate finance: Insufficient funding, accessibility issues for developing nations, and lack of transparency.
India's role: Advocating for climate justice, leading by example in renewable energy adoption, and promoting sustainable practices.
Global cooperation: Need for stronger commitments from developed countries and innovative financing mechanisms.
articles.imageDescription: Hands holding a sapling, symbolizing environmental protection and growth.
articles.trivia: The first COP (Conference of the Parties) meeting was held in Berlin in 1995.

articles.headline: Government Unveils Roadmap for Expanding Digital Public Infrastructure Access Across Rural India
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched a comprehensive roadmap for enhancing digital public infrastructure (DPI) in rural areas.
• The plan focuses on expanding broadband connectivity, promoting digital literacy, and ensuring access to online government services.
• Aims to bridge the digital divide and foster inclusive growth through digital empowerment for all citizens.
• Initiatives include public Wi-Fi hotspots, community digital centers, and localized content development.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: Examples of DPI in India include Aadhaar, UPI (Unified Payments Interface), and DigiLocker.
The BharatNet project aims to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats.
DPI refers to shared digital systems built on open standards that enable a wide range of public and private sector services.
mainsPoints: Benefits: Financial inclusion, access to education and healthcare, e-governance, economic opportunities, and empowerment of vulnerable sections.
Challenges: Infrastructure gaps, affordability, digital literacy barriers, data privacy concerns, and cybersecurity risks.
Government initiatives: Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA) and Common Service Centres (CSCs).
Way forward: Public-private partnerships, development of local language content, and a robust data protection framework.
articles.imageDescription: People using digital devices in a rural setting.
articles.trivia: India's UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is one of the most successful DPIs globally.

articles.headline: Ministry of Home Affairs Reviews Progress on Integrated Border Management System
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) held a high-level meeting to review the implementation of the Integrated Border Management System (IBMS).
• The system aims to enhance surveillance, intelligence gathering, and response capabilities along India's land and coastal borders.
• Focus areas include the deployment of advanced technology, improved coordination among security agencies, and infrastructure development.
• The review highlighted significant progress in deploying smart fences and drone technology in sensitive border sectors.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: India shares land borders with 7 countries and has a long coastline.
Key Border Guarding Forces include BSF (Pakistan, Bangladesh), ITBP (China), and SSB (Nepal, Bhutan).
IBMS integrates various technologies like radars, sensors, cameras, and communication systems for comprehensive border monitoring.
mainsPoints: Challenges: Porous borders, diverse terrain, cross-border terrorism, smuggling, and illegal immigration.
Benefits of IBMS: Enhanced deterrence, quicker response times, reduced human casualties, and improved intelligence gathering.
Technological integration: Use of AI and machine learning for predictive analysis and facial recognition.
Human element: Importance of training and equipping border personnel, alongside improving their living conditions.
articles.imageDescription: A border fence with surveillance cameras.
articles.trivia: The Border Security Force (BSF) was established in 1965.

articles.headline: Review Meeting Held on Progress of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation in Higher Education
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a review of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's implementation in higher education institutions.
• Discussions focused on the adoption of multidisciplinary approaches, credit transfer mechanisms, and vocational education integration.
• Challenges identified include faculty training, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable access to quality education.
• The government reiterated its commitment to transforming India into a global knowledge superpower through the NEP.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
Key recommendations include a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure and multidisciplinary higher education with flexible curricula.
UGC (University Grants Commission) and AICTE (All India Council for Technical Education) are key regulatory bodies in higher education.
mainsPoints: Aims of NEP: Holistic development, critical thinking, skill development, promoting Indian languages, and internationalization of education.
Challenges in implementation: Funding, resistance from faculty, infrastructure gaps, the digital divide, and standardization issues.
Impact on employability: How vocational training and skill integration can improve graduate employability.
Equity and inclusion: Ensuring NEP benefits all sections of society, including marginalized groups, through equitable access.
articles.imageDescription: Students in a modern university classroom setting.
articles.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's education policy.

articles.headline: Government Announces New Incentives to Boost Domestic Manufacturing of Renewable Energy Components
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy unveiled a new scheme offering production-linked incentives (PLI) for domestic manufacturing of solar PV modules and advanced battery storage.
• Aims to reduce India's dependence on imports, create jobs, and achieve ambitious renewable energy targets.
• The move is expected to attract significant private investment in the renewable energy sector, fostering self-reliance.
• Part of India's broader strategy to enhance energy security and combat climate change.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: India's target for renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) is 500 GW by 2030.
PLI schemes are government initiatives designed to boost domestic manufacturing and exports.
Key government initiatives in renewable energy include the National Solar Mission and PM-KUSUM.
mainsPoints: Benefits of domestic manufacturing: Job creation, reduced import bill, technological self-reliance, and strategic autonomy.
Challenges: Land acquisition, financing, grid integration issues, and raw material sourcing.
Role in energy transition: Decarbonization of the economy and meeting international climate commitments.
Future outlook: Potential for India to become a global hub for green energy manufacturing.
articles.imageDescription: Solar panels on a large field, generating electricity.
articles.trivia: The International Solar Alliance (ISA) was jointly launched by India and France.

articles.headline: India Releases Draft National Framework for Ethical Artificial Intelligence
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) released a draft national framework for ethical Artificial Intelligence (AI).
• Aims to ensure the responsible development and deployment of AI technologies, addressing concerns like bias, transparency, and accountability.
• The framework emphasizes human-centric AI, privacy protection, and data security in its guidelines.
• Public feedback is invited to finalize the guidelines, setting a precedent for AI governance in India.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: AI is a branch of computer science that aims to create intelligent machines that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence.
Examples of AI applications include facial recognition, natural language processing, and recommendation systems.
Key principles of ethical AI often include fairness, transparency, privacy, and accountability.
mainsPoints: Benefits of AI: Economic growth, healthcare advancements, smart cities, and improved public services.
Ethical concerns: Bias and discrimination in algorithms, potential for job displacement, surveillance, autonomous weapons, and data misuse.
Need for regulation: Ensuring human oversight, establishing legal liability, and building public trust in AI systems.
India's approach: Balancing innovation with responsible deployment, and leveraging AI for social good.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized brain with interconnected nodes, representing AI.
articles.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

articles.headline: National Mission Launched to Combat Urban Flooding in Major Indian Cities
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs launched a comprehensive National Mission for Urban Flooding Mitigation.
• The mission focuses on improving drainage infrastructure, implementing early warning systems, and promoting sustainable urban planning practices.
• Aims to reduce the vulnerability of Indian cities to increasing instances of heavy rainfall and flash floods.
• Involves inter-agency coordination, community participation, and adoption of nature-based solutions.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc.
geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Urban flooding is distinct from rural flooding due to rapid runoff, impervious surfaces, and blocked drainage systems.
Factors contributing to urban floods include encroachment on natural water bodies, inadequate drainage, and climate change-induced extreme weather.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) provides guidelines for urban flood management.
mainsPoints: Causes of urban flooding: Unplanned urbanization, defunct drainage systems, climate change-induced extreme rainfall, and encroachment of wetlands.
Impacts: Economic losses, health hazards, disruption of essential services, and loss of life.
Mitigation strategies: Integrated stormwater management, green infrastructure, community awareness, robust early warning systems, and effective land-use planning.
Role of local bodies: Strengthening municipal capacity and improving governance for effective urban flood management.
articles.imageDescription: Waterlogged city street after heavy rainfall.
articles.trivia: Mumbai's Mithi River, now largely concretized, was a major cause of the 2005 Mumbai floods.

articles.headline: India Reaffirms Commitment to UN Peacekeeping, Calls for Enhanced Mandate Protection
articles.explainer:
• India, at a UN Security Council debate, reiterated its steadfast commitment to UN Peacekeeping Operations (UNPKOs).
• Called for stronger mandates for peacekeepers, including enhanced protection and sufficient resources to fulfill their duties.
• Highlighted India's historical and significant contribution as one of the largest troop-contributing countries globally.
• Emphasized the need for political solutions and addressing the root causes of conflicts to ensure lasting peace.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: India is one of the largest cumulative troop contributors to UN peacekeeping missions.
Indian peacekeepers have served in various missions across Africa, Asia, and Europe.
The UN Security Council is responsible for authorizing peacekeeping missions.
The 'Vishwa Mitra' (Friends of the World) is a motto associated with Indian peacekeepers.
mainsPoints: India's contribution: Historical legacy, provision of medical support, deployment of women peacekeepers, and capacity building for host nations.
Challenges for UNPKOs: Complex mandates, asymmetric threats, lack of resources, insufficient political will, and ensuring the safety of peacekeepers.
Reforms needed: Strengthening mandates, better training, technological upgrades, and accountability for crimes against peacekeepers.
Role of India in UNSC reform: Advocating for permanent membership to reflect current geopolitical realities.
articles.imageDescription: UN peacekeepers in blue helmets on patrol.
articles.trivia: India was the first country to deploy an all-women contingent to a UN peacekeeping mission (Liberia, 2007).

articles.headline: Indian Pharmaceutical Sector Records Robust Growth, Focus on R&D and Export Diversification
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers reported significant growth in India's pharmaceutical sector in the current fiscal year.
• Growth is attributed to increased domestic demand, robust exports, and government support for research and development (R&D).
• Emphasis on diversifying export markets and strengthening India's position as a global pharmaceutical hub.
• Challenges include regulatory harmonization, intellectual property rights protection, and competition from other global players.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: India is known as the 'pharmacy of the world' due to its large production of generic drugs.
The Department of Pharmaceuticals, under the Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, oversees the sector.
The PLI (Production Linked Incentive) scheme for pharmaceuticals aims to boost domestic manufacturing of key starting materials (KSMs) and APIs.
mainsPoints: Strengths: Cost-effective manufacturing, skilled workforce, strong R&D base, and a large domestic market.
Opportunities: Growth in biosimilars, vaccines, contract research and manufacturing services (CRAMS), and digital health solutions.
Challenges: Regulatory hurdles, quality control standards, IPR enforcement, and dependence on foreign sources for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs).
Government policies: 'Pharma Vision 2020', support for R&D, and promotion of medical tourism.
articles.imageDescription: Various medicine bottles and capsules on a table.
articles.trivia: India supplies over 50% of the global demand for various vaccines.

articles.headline: ASI Unveils New Initiatives for Digital Documentation and Preservation of Ancient Sites
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced new initiatives leveraging digital technologies for the documentation and preservation of India's cultural heritage.
• Includes 3D mapping, laser scanning, and virtual reality tours of ancient monuments and archaeological sites.
• Aims to make heritage accessible to a wider audience and enhance conservation efforts efficiently.
• Part of a broader strategy to protect and promote India's rich historical legacy for future generations.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: ASI is an attached office of the Ministry of Culture, Government of India.
It is responsible for archaeological research and the protection of cultural heritage.
The Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958, governs the protection of monuments.
mainsPoints: Importance of heritage: National identity, tourism promotion, educational value, and economic development.
Challenges in preservation: Vandalism, encroachment, environmental degradation, lack of funding, and limited manpower.
Role of technology: Enhanced documentation, remote monitoring, public engagement, educational outreach, and disaster preparedness.
Community involvement: Promoting local participation and awareness in heritage conservation efforts.
articles.imageDescription: A historical monument being digitally scanned.
articles.trivia: India has 42 UNESCO World Heritage Sites as of 2024.

articles.headline: Government Prioritizes Public Health Infrastructure Upgradation Under Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced a major push for upgrading public health infrastructure, integrating it with the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
• Focus on digitizing health records, strengthening telemedicine services, and enhancing diagnostic capabilities in rural areas.
• Aims to provide universal and equitable access to quality healthcare services across the nation.
• This strategic move builds upon the lessons learned from recent public health crises and aims for resilience.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABDM aims to create a national digital health ecosystem.
Key components of ABDM include Health ID, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), and Health Facility Registry (HFR).
Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) provides health insurance coverage to vulnerable families.
mainsPoints: Challenges in Indian healthcare: Rural-urban divide, shortage of skilled personnel, inadequate infrastructure, and high out-of-pocket expenditure.
Role of digital health: Improving accessibility, efficiency, data-driven policy making, and reducing healthcare costs.
Ethical concerns: Data privacy, security breaches, and digital literacy barriers for effective adoption among the populace.
Way forward: Public-private partnerships, strengthening community health workers, and a greater focus on preventive care.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor consulting a patient via telemedicine.
articles.trivia: India's first National Health Policy was formulated in 1983.

articles.headline: Government Boosts Agri-Tech Startups with New Fund and Incubation Support
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched a new fund and incubation program to support agri-tech startups.
• Aims to promote innovation in agriculture, including precision farming, supply chain management, and sustainable practices.
• The initiative seeks to address challenges like climate change, soil degradation, and market access for farmers.
• Expected to drive modernization and efficiency in the agricultural sector, enhancing farmer incomes.
gsSubTopics: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country
e-technology in the aid of farmers
Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices
prelimsPoints: Agri-tech involves the use of technology in agriculture to improve efficiency, productivity, and sustainability.
Examples include IoT sensors for soil monitoring, drones for crop spraying, and AI for yield prediction.
Government schemes like the Agri-startup challenge and Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY) support agricultural innovation.
mainsPoints: Benefits: Increased farmer income, enhanced food security, efficient resource use, climate resilience, and reduced post-harvest losses.
Challenges: Digital literacy among farmers, capital investment requirements, infrastructure gaps, market access, and data privacy concerns.
Role of government: Policy support, funding mechanisms, R&D promotion, and creating an enabling ecosystem for agri-tech.
Impact on rural economy: Job creation, value addition in agricultural produce, and attracting youth to the agriculture sector.
articles.imageDescription: A drone flying over a field for agricultural monitoring.
articles.trivia: India is the second-largest producer of agricultural products globally.

highlights: Nationwide Trade Union Protests Against New Labour Codes Intensify, Raising Concerns Over Worker Rights.
Supreme Court Upholds Federal Principles in Landmark Inter-State Water Dispute Verdict.
India's Q2 GDP Growth Exceeds Expectations, Driven by Manufacturing and Services Sectors.
ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Disaster Management.
India Proposes New Global Climate Change Adaptation Fund, Emphasizing Equitable Burden-Sharing.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the new Labour Codes in India:
1. The Code on Wages consolidates laws relating to wages and bonus payments.
2. The Industrial Relations Code allows establishments employing up to 300 workers to retrench without government permission.
3. Trade unions have largely supported the new codes, citing ease of doing business.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. The Code on Wages, 2019, consolidates four existing labour laws relating to wages and bonus payments. The Industrial Relations Code, 2020, allows establishments with up to 300 workers to retrench without government permission (previously 100). Statement 3 is incorrect; trade unions have largely opposed the new codes, citing concerns over dilution of worker rights and weakening of collective bargaining power.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to inter-state water disputes in India, consider the following statements:
1. Water is a subject in the Union List of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution.
2. Article 262 empowers the Parliament to legislate on the adjudication of inter-state river disputes.
3. The Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, provides for the establishment of tribunals for such disputes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. Water (Entry 17) is primarily a subject in the State List, though Parliament has powers under Entry 56 of the Union List for the regulation and development of inter-state rivers and river valleys. Statements 2 and 3 are correct. Article 262 specifically deals with the adjudication of disputes relating to waters of inter-State rivers or river valleys, and the Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, was enacted under this provision.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes the primary utility of Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) launched by ISRO?
options: (a) Providing high-speed internet connectivity to remote areas.
(b) Facilitating inter-planetary missions and deep space exploration.
(c) Gathering data for disaster management, urban planning, and agricultural monitoring.
(d) Supporting global positioning systems and navigation services.
prelimsMocks.answer: (c) Gathering data for disaster management, urban planning, and agricultural monitoring.
prelimsMocks.explanation: Earth Observation Satellites (EOS) are specifically designed to monitor Earth's surface and atmosphere. Their primary utility lies in applications such as disaster management (flood mapping, forest fire detection), urban planning (land-use mapping, infrastructure development), and agricultural monitoring (crop health, yield estimation). Options (a), (b), and (d) describe functions of communication satellites, deep space missions, and navigation satellites, respectively.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following is/are considered examples of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in India?
1. Aadhaar
2. Unified Payments Interface (UPI)
3. DigiLocker
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three, Aadhaar (digital identity), Unified Payments Interface (UPI - digital payments), and DigiLocker (digital document wallet), are prominent examples of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in India. They are open, interoperable platforms that enable various public and private sector services, fostering inclusion and efficiency.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding India's renewable energy sector and related government initiatives:
1. India aims to achieve 500 GW of renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) by 2030.
2. Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes are used to boost domestic manufacturing in sectors like solar PV modules.
3. The International Solar Alliance (ISA) was initiated by India and the USA.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statements 1 and 2 are correct. India has a target of achieving 500 GW of non-fossil energy capacity by 2030, which includes renewable energy. PLI schemes are indeed a key government strategy to promote domestic manufacturing in various sectors, including solar PV modules and advanced chemistry cell (ACC) battery storage. Statement 3 is incorrect; the International Solar Alliance (ISA) was jointly launched by India and France, not the USA.

mainsMocks.question: "The recent nationwide protests by trade unions against new labour codes highlight a fundamental tension between ease of doing business and worker protection." Discuss this statement in the context of India's labour reforms, evaluating the potential benefits and challenges for both employers and employees. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; GS3: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the new labour codes and the context of protests. Discuss arguments for the reforms, focusing on ease of doing business, attracting investment, and formalizing the economy. Subsequently, present arguments against the reforms from the perspective of worker protection, highlighting concerns over dilution of rights, increased precarity, and weakening of trade unions. Conclude by suggesting a balanced approach that ensures both economic growth and social justice for sustainable labour relations.

mainsMocks.question: Analyze the significance of Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) in fostering inclusive growth and governance in India. What are the key challenges in expanding DPI access, particularly in rural areas, and suggest measures to overcome them? (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors; Inclusive growth and issues arising from it; GS3: Infrastructure.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by defining Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI) and providing examples like Aadhaar, UPI, and DigiLocker. Explain how DPI contributes to inclusive growth (e.g., financial inclusion, access to education and healthcare, e-governance) and improves governance. Identify key challenges in expanding DPI access, especially in rural areas, such as infrastructure gaps, digital literacy barriers, affordability, and data privacy concerns. Finally, suggest comprehensive measures to overcome these challenges, including initiatives like BharatNet, public-private partnerships, localized content, and robust cybersecurity frameworks.

mainsMocks.question: Urban flooding has emerged as a recurring challenge for Indian cities, exacerbated by climate change. Critically examine the factors contributing to urban floods and propose comprehensive mitigation strategies, integrating both infrastructure and policy measures. (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS1: Important Geophysical phenomena; GS3: Disaster and disaster management.
mainsMocks.approach: Define urban flooding and differentiate it from rural flooding. Analyze the primary factors contributing to its occurrence in Indian cities, such as unplanned urbanization, concretization of natural surfaces, encroachment on water bodies, inadequate drainage systems, and the impact of climate change-induced extreme rainfall. Propose a range of comprehensive mitigation strategies, including both hard infrastructure solutions (e.g., integrated stormwater management, improved drainage) and policy measures (e.g., green infrastructure, early warning systems, strict land-use planning, community participation, and strengthening local governance).

quickBytes.text: The Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, empowers the Central government to set up tribunals for water disputes.
The National Statistical Office (NSO) is responsible for releasing official macroeconomic statistics in India.
India's 'Look East Policy' was rebranded as 'Act East Policy' in 2014, with a greater focus on strategic engagement.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) is known as ISRO's 'workhorse' for launching Earth Observation Satellites.
The Green Climate Fund (GCF) is a global fund created to support developing countries' climate change efforts.
The Border Security Force (BSF) guards India's land borders with Pakistan and Bangladesh.
The National Education Policy 2020 aims to increase the Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER) in higher education to 50% by 2035.
India's commitment to achieving Net Zero emissions by 2070 was announced at COP26 in Glasgow.
The term 'Deepfake' refers to synthetic media in which a person in an existing image or video is replaced.
The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) was founded in 1861 by Alexander Cunningham.

GS1.headline: National Mission Launched to Combat Urban Flooding in Major Indian Cities
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs launched a comprehensive National Mission for Urban Flooding Mitigation.
• The mission focuses on improving drainage infrastructure, implementing early warning systems, and promoting sustainable urban planning practices.
• Aims to reduce the vulnerability of Indian cities to increasing instances of heavy rainfall and flash floods.
• Involves inter-agency coordination, community participation, and adoption of nature-based solutions.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc.
geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes
Disaster and disaster management
prelimsPoints: Urban flooding is distinct from rural flooding due to rapid runoff, impervious surfaces, and blocked drainage systems.
Factors contributing to urban floods include encroachment on natural water bodies, inadequate drainage, and climate change-induced extreme weather.
The National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) provides guidelines for urban flood management.
mainsPoints: Causes of urban flooding: Unplanned urbanization, defunct drainage systems, climate change-induced extreme rainfall, and encroachment of wetlands.
Impacts: Economic losses, health hazards, disruption of essential services, and loss of life.
Mitigation strategies: Integrated stormwater management, green infrastructure, community awareness, robust early warning systems, and effective land-use planning.
Role of local bodies: Strengthening municipal capacity and improving governance for effective urban flood management.
GS1.imageDescription: Waterlogged city street after heavy rainfall.
GS1.trivia: Mumbai's Mithi River, now largely concretized, was a major cause of the 2005 Mumbai floods.

GS1.headline: ASI Unveils New Initiatives for Digital Documentation and Preservation of Ancient Sites
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced new initiatives leveraging digital technologies for the documentation and preservation of India's cultural heritage.
• Includes 3D mapping, laser scanning, and virtual reality tours of ancient monuments and archaeological sites.
• Aims to make heritage accessible to a wider audience and enhance conservation efforts efficiently.
• Part of a broader strategy to protect and promote India's rich historical legacy for future generations.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: ASI is an attached office of the Ministry of Culture, Government of India.
It is responsible for archaeological research and the protection of cultural heritage.
The Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958, governs the protection of monuments.
mainsPoints: Importance of heritage: National identity, tourism promotion, educational value, and economic development.
Challenges in preservation: Vandalism, encroachment, environmental degradation, lack of funding, and limited manpower.
Role of technology: Enhanced documentation, remote monitoring, public engagement, educational outreach, and disaster preparedness.
Community involvement: Promoting local participation and awareness in heritage conservation efforts.
GS1.imageDescription: A historical monument being digitally scanned.
GS1.trivia: India has 42 UNESCO World Heritage Sites as of 2024.

GS2.headline: Nationwide Trade Union Protests Against New Labour Codes Intensify
GS2.explainer:
• Ten major trade unions orchestrated a nationwide protest against the new labour codes.
• Demands include the withdrawal of the new labour codes and stronger protection of workers' rights.
• Concerns raised over the dilution of worker protections, ease of hire and fire, and reduced collective bargaining power.
• The government reiterates that reforms aim to improve ease of doing business and formalize the labour sector.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Social empowerment
prelimsPoints: The four new labour codes are: Code on Wages, Industrial Relations Code, Code on Social Security, and Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code.
These codes aim to consolidate 29 central labour laws.
Key provisions causing concern include higher thresholds for retrenchment and changes in strike notice periods.
mainsPoints: Arguments for reforms: Ease of doing business, attracting investment, formalizing the economy, creating employment.
Arguments against reforms: Dilution of worker rights, increased precarity, weakening of trade unions, potential for exploitation, impact on social security.
Impact on gig economy: Discussion on how the new codes address or fail to address gig workers.
Way forward: Need for a balanced approach, social dialogue, and ensuring minimum wage and social security for all.
GS2.imageDescription: Workers holding placards and banners during a nationwide protest against labour codes.
GS2.trivia: The first comprehensive labour law in India was the Factories Act of 1881.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Federal Principles in Inter-State Water Dispute Verdict
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court delivered a landmark judgment clarifying the Centre-State roles in inter-state water resource management.
• The verdict emphasized the constitutional scheme of federalism and cooperative federalism in resolving disputes.
• It is expected to set a precedent for future inter-state resource allocation issues across the country.
• The judgment highlighted the role of tribunals and the Supreme Court's appellate jurisdiction in such matters.
gsSubTopics: Functions and responsibilities of the Union and the States, issues and challenges pertaining to the federal structure
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions
prelimsPoints: Article 262 of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to legislate on inter-state river disputes.
The Inter-State Water Disputes Act, 1956, provides for the adjudication of such disputes.
Water is a State subject (Entry 17, State List), but Parliament has powers for regulation and development of inter-state rivers (Entry 56, Union List).
mainsPoints: Challenges in water governance: Climate change impact, increasing demand, uneven distribution, political complexities.
Role of cooperative federalism: How states and centre can collaborate for sustainable water management.
Significance of the verdict: Strengthening federal structure, ensuring equitable resource distribution, reducing inter-state conflicts.
Recommendations: Need for national water policy, basin-level management, and enhanced data sharing.
GS2.imageDescription: Gavel and Constitution of India symbolizing legal judgment.
GS2.trivia: India has over 20 major inter-state river basins.

GS2.headline: India and France Strengthen Strategic Partnership in Annual Dialogue, Focus on Defence and Space
GS2.explainer:
• The annual India-France Strategic Dialogue concluded, reaffirming the deep bilateral ties between the two nations.
• Key discussions revolved around enhancing defense cooperation, including co-development and co-production of military hardware.
• Space collaboration, particularly in satellite technology and space situational awareness, was a prominent agenda item.
• Both nations expressed a strong commitment to a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific region.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
prelimsPoints: India and France elevated their relationship to a 'Strategic Partnership' in 1998.
Key defense deals between the two nations include the procurement of Rafale jets and Scorpene submarines.
ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) and CNES (French Space Agency) have a long-standing collaboration.
France is a permanent member of the UN Security Council.
mainsPoints: Pillars of cooperation: Defence, space, nuclear energy, counter-terrorism, and climate change.
Significance for India: Access to advanced technology, diversification of defense suppliers, strategic autonomy, and balancing China's influence in the Indo-Pacific.
Challenges: Geopolitical shifts, economic competition, and ensuring timely project delivery.
Future outlook: Deepening cooperation in emerging technologies and maritime security.
GS2.imageDescription: Indian and French flags side-by-side, symbolizing diplomatic ties.
GS2.trivia: France was the first country to establish a strategic partnership with India.

GS2.headline: Review Meeting Held on Progress of National Education Policy 2020 Implementation in Higher Education
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Education conducted a review of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's implementation in higher education institutions.
• Discussions focused on the adoption of multidisciplinary approaches, credit transfer mechanisms, and vocational education integration.
• Challenges identified include faculty training, infrastructure development, and ensuring equitable access to quality education.
• The government reiterated its commitment to transforming India into a global knowledge superpower through the NEP.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
prelimsPoints: NEP 2020 replaced the 1986 National Policy on Education.
Key recommendations include a 5+3+3+4 curricular structure and multidisciplinary higher education with flexible curricula.
UGC (University Grants Commission) and AICTE (All India Council for Technical Education) are key regulatory bodies in higher education.
mainsPoints: Aims of NEP: Holistic development, critical thinking, skill development, promoting Indian languages, and internationalization of education.
Challenges in implementation: Funding, resistance from faculty, infrastructure gaps, the digital divide, and standardization issues.
Impact on employability: How vocational training and skill integration can improve graduate employability.
Equity and inclusion: Ensuring NEP benefits all sections of society, including marginalized groups, through equitable access.
GS2.imageDescription: Students in a modern university classroom setting.
GS2.trivia: The Kothari Commission (1964-66) was instrumental in shaping India's education policy.

GS2.headline: India Reaffirms Commitment to UN Peacekeeping, Calls for Enhanced Mandate Protection
GS2.explainer:
• India, at a UN Security Council debate, reiterated its steadfast commitment to UN Peacekeeping Operations (UNPKOs).
• Called for stronger mandates for peacekeepers, including enhanced protection and sufficient resources to fulfill their duties.
• Highlighted India's historical and significant contribution as one of the largest troop-contributing countries globally.
• Emphasized the need for political solutions and addressing the root causes of conflicts to ensure lasting peace.
gsSubTopics: Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: India is one of the largest cumulative troop contributors to UN peacekeeping missions.
Indian peacekeepers have served in various missions across Africa, Asia, and Europe.
The UN Security Council is responsible for authorizing peacekeeping missions.
The 'Vishwa Mitra' (Friends of the World) is a motto associated with Indian peacekeepers.
mainsPoints: India's contribution: Historical legacy, provision of medical support, deployment of women peacekeepers, and capacity building for host nations.
Challenges for UNPKOs: Complex mandates, asymmetric threats, lack of resources, insufficient political will, and ensuring the safety of peacekeepers.
Reforms needed: Strengthening mandates, better training, technological upgrades, and accountability for crimes against peacekeepers.
Role of India in UNSC reform: Advocating for permanent membership to reflect current geopolitical realities.
GS2.imageDescription: UN peacekeepers in blue helmets on patrol.
GS2.trivia: India was the first country to deploy an all-women contingent to a UN peacekeeping mission (Liberia, 2007).

GS2.headline: Government Prioritizes Public Health Infrastructure Upgradation Under Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission
GS2.explainer:
• The Ministry of Health and Family Welfare announced a major push for upgrading public health infrastructure, integrating it with the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM).
• Focus on digitizing health records, strengthening telemedicine services, and enhancing diagnostic capabilities in rural areas.
• Aims to provide universal and equitable access to quality healthcare services across the nation.
• This strategic move builds upon the lessons learned from recent public health crises and aims for resilience.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
GS2.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: ABDM aims to create a national digital health ecosystem.
Key components of ABDM include Health ID, Healthcare Professionals Registry (HPR), and Health Facility Registry (HFR).
Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (AB-PMJAY) provides health insurance coverage to vulnerable families.
mainsPoints: Challenges in Indian healthcare: Rural-urban divide, shortage of skilled personnel, inadequate infrastructure, and high out-of-pocket expenditure.
Role of digital health: Improving accessibility, efficiency, data-driven policy making, and reducing healthcare costs.
Ethical concerns: Data privacy, security breaches, and digital literacy barriers for effective adoption among the populace.
Way forward: Public-private partnerships, strengthening community health workers, and a greater focus on preventive care.
GS2.imageDescription: A doctor consulting a patient via telemedicine.
GS2.trivia: India's first National Health Policy was formulated in 1983.

GS3.headline: India's Q2 GDP Growth Exceeds Expectations, Driven by Manufacturing and Services
GS3.explainer:
• The National Statistical Office (NSO) released the Q2 (July-September) GDP figures for FY 2025-26.
• Growth rate surpassed analyst predictions, primarily fueled by robust performance in manufacturing and services sectors.
• Private consumption and investment also showed positive trends, indicating a broad-based economic recovery.
• Challenges remain in managing inflation and navigating global economic uncertainties, despite the strong performance.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
Investment models
prelimsPoints: GDP is the total monetary or market value of all the finished goods and services produced within a country's borders in a specific time period.
NSO, under the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI), is responsible for releasing GDP data.
The components of GDP typically include Consumption (C), Investment (I), Government Spending (G), and Net Exports (NX).
mainsPoints: Drivers of growth: Government capital expenditure, Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes, digital transformation, and services exports.
Sectoral analysis: Performance and contribution of agriculture, industry, and services sectors to overall growth.
Challenges: Global slowdown, supply chain disruptions, unemployment, income inequality, and inflation management.
Policy implications: Fiscal policy, monetary policy, and structural reforms needed for sustained and inclusive growth.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphical representation of economic growth with upward arrows.
GS3.trivia: India is currently the 5th largest economy in the world by nominal GDP.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Advanced Earth Observation Satellite for Disaster Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched a new-generation Earth Observation Satellite (EOS) from Sriharikota.
• The satellite is equipped with high-resolution imaging capabilities crucial for disaster management, urban planning, and agricultural monitoring.
• It will provide real-time data for tracking weather patterns, forest fires, and flood mapping.
• This launch further strengthens India's indigenous capabilities in space-based remote sensing and data collection.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Space
prelimsPoints: ISRO is India's national space agency, headquartered in Bengaluru.
PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle) and GSLV (Geosynchronous Satellite Launch Vehicle) are ISRO's primary launch vehicles.
Earth observation satellites are used for monitoring Earth's physical, chemical, and biological systems.
Sriharikota is home to the Satish Dhawan Space Centre (SDSC), India's primary spaceport.
mainsPoints: Applications: Agriculture (crop health, yield estimation), urban planning, water resource management, climate studies, and border surveillance.
Contribution to disaster management: Early warning systems, damage assessment, and relief coordination.
Self-reliance in space: Reducing dependence on foreign satellites and boosting the domestic space industry.
Challenges: Space debris, cybersecurity of space assets, and international competition in the space sector.
GS3.imageDescription: A rocket launching into space from a launchpad.
GS3.trivia: India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched in 1975.

GS3.headline: India Proposes New Global Climate Change Adaptation Fund at International Forum
GS3.explainer:
• India, at a major international climate forum, advocated for the creation of a dedicated global fund for climate change adaptation.
• The proposal highlighted the disproportionate impact of climate change on developing nations and their limited resources for adaptation measures.
• Emphasized the need for equitable burden-sharing and technology transfer from developed to developing countries.
• The fund aims to support projects in areas like resilient infrastructure, sustainable agriculture, and early warning systems.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Important International institutions, agencies and fora, their structure, mandate
prelimsPoints: The Adaptation Fund was established under the Kyoto Protocol.
The Green Climate Fund (GCF) is another major fund under the UNFCCC (United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change).
Adaptation refers to adjustments in ecological, social, or economic systems in response to actual or expected climatic stimuli and their effects.
mainsPoints: Importance of adaptation: Reducing vulnerability, protecting livelihoods, and ensuring food security in the face of climate change.
Challenges in climate finance: Insufficient funding, accessibility issues for developing nations, and lack of transparency.
India's role: Advocating for climate justice, leading by example in renewable energy adoption, and promoting sustainable practices.
Global cooperation: Need for stronger commitments from developed countries and innovative financing mechanisms.
GS3.imageDescription: Hands holding a sapling, symbolizing environmental protection and growth.
GS3.trivia: The first COP (Conference of the Parties) meeting was held in Berlin in 1995.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils Roadmap for Expanding Digital Public Infrastructure Access Across Rural India
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) launched a comprehensive roadmap for enhancing digital public infrastructure (DPI) in rural areas.
• The plan focuses on expanding broadband connectivity, promoting digital literacy, and ensuring access to online government services.
• Aims to bridge the digital divide and foster inclusive growth through digital empowerment for all citizens.
• Initiatives include public Wi-Fi hotspots, community digital centers, and localized content development.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
prelimsPoints: Examples of DPI in India include Aadhaar, UPI (Unified Payments Interface), and DigiLocker.
The BharatNet project aims to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats.
DPI refers to shared digital systems built on open standards that enable a wide range of public and private sector services.
mainsPoints: Benefits: Financial inclusion, access to education and healthcare, e-governance, economic opportunities, and empowerment of vulnerable sections.
Challenges: Infrastructure gaps, affordability, digital literacy barriers, data privacy concerns, and cybersecurity risks.
Government initiatives: Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan (PMGDISHA) and Common Service Centres (CSCs).
Way forward: Public-private partnerships, development of local language content, and a robust data protection framework.
GS3.imageDescription: People using digital devices in a rural setting.
GS3.trivia: India's UPI (Unified Payments Interface) is one of the most successful DPIs globally.

GS3.headline: Ministry of Home Affairs Reviews Progress on Integrated Border Management System
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) held a high-level meeting to review the implementation of the Integrated Border Management System (IBMS).
• The system aims to enhance surveillance, intelligence gathering, and response capabilities along India's land and coastal borders.
• Focus areas include the deployment of advanced technology, improved coordination among security agencies, and infrastructure development.
• The review highlighted significant progress in deploying smart fences and drone technology in sensitive border sectors.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: India shares land borders with 7 countries and has a long coastline.
Key Border Guarding Forces include BSF (Pakistan, Bangladesh), ITBP (China), and SSB (Nepal, Bhutan).
IBMS integrates various technologies like radars, sensors, cameras, and communication systems for comprehensive border monitoring.
mainsPoints: Challenges: Porous borders, diverse terrain, cross-border terrorism, smuggling, and illegal immigration.
Benefits of IBMS: Enhanced deterrence, quicker response times, reduced human casualties, and improved intelligence gathering.
Technological integration: Use of AI and machine learning for predictive analysis and facial recognition.
Human element: Importance of training and equipping border personnel, alongside improving their living conditions.
GS3.imageDescription: A border fence with surveillance cameras.
GS3.trivia: The Border Security Force (BSF) was established in 1965.

GS3.headline: Government Announces New Incentives to Boost Domestic Manufacturing of Renewable Energy Components
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy unveiled a new scheme offering production-linked incentives (PLI) for domestic manufacturing of solar PV modules and advanced battery storage.
• Aims to reduce India's dependence on imports, create jobs, and achieve ambitious renewable energy targets.
• The move is expected to attract significant private investment in the renewable energy sector, fostering self-reliance.
• Part of India's broader strategy to enhance energy security and combat climate change.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: India's target for renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) is 500 GW by 2030.
PLI schemes are government initiatives designed to boost domestic manufacturing and exports.
Key government initiatives in renewable energy include the National Solar Mission and PM-KUSUM.
mainsPoints: Benefits of domestic manufacturing: Job creation, reduced import bill, technological self-reliance, and strategic autonomy.
Challenges: Land acquisition, financing, grid integration issues, and raw material sourcing.
Role in energy transition: Decarbonization of the economy and meeting international climate commitments.
Future outlook: Potential for India to become a global hub for green energy manufacturing.
GS3.imageDescription: Solar panels on a large field, generating electricity.
GS3.trivia: The International Solar Alliance (ISA) was jointly launched by India and France.

GS3.headline: India Releases Draft National Framework for Ethical Artificial Intelligence
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) released a draft national framework for ethical Artificial Intelligence (AI).
• Aims to ensure the responsible development and deployment of AI technologies, addressing concerns like bias, transparency, and accountability.
• The framework emphasizes human-centric AI, privacy protection, and data security in its guidelines.
• Public feedback is invited to finalize the guidelines, setting a precedent for AI governance in India.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors
prelimsPoints: AI is a branch of computer science that aims to create intelligent machines that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence.
Examples of AI applications include facial recognition, natural language processing, and recommendation systems.
Key principles of ethical AI often include fairness, transparency, privacy, and accountability.
mainsPoints: Benefits of AI: Economic growth, healthcare advancements, smart cities, and improved public services.
Ethical concerns: Bias and discrimination in algorithms, potential for job displacement, surveillance, autonomous weapons, and data misuse.
Need for regulation: Ensuring human oversight, establishing legal liability, and building public trust in AI systems.
India's approach: Balancing innovation with responsible deployment, and leveraging AI for social good.
GS3.imageDescription: A stylized brain with interconnected nodes, representing AI.
GS3.trivia: The term 'Artificial Intelligence' was coined by John McCarthy in 1956.

GS3.headline: Indian Pharmaceutical Sector Records Robust Growth, Focus on R&D and Export Diversification
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers reported significant growth in India's pharmaceutical sector in the current fiscal year.
• Growth is attributed to increased domestic demand, robust exports, and government support for research and development (R&D).
• Emphasis on diversifying export markets and strengthening India's position as a global pharmaceutical hub.
• Challenges include regulatory harmonization, intellectual property rights protection, and competition from other global players.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
prelimsPoints: India is known as the 'pharmacy of the world' due to its large production of generic drugs.
The Department of Pharmaceuticals, under the Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers, oversees the sector.
The PLI (Production Linked Incentive) scheme for pharmaceuticals aims to boost domestic manufacturing of key starting materials (KSMs) and APIs.
mainsPoints: Strengths: Cost-effective manufacturing, skilled workforce, strong R&D base, and a large domestic market.
Opportunities: Growth in biosimilars, vaccines, contract research and manufacturing services (CRAMS), and digital health solutions.
Challenges: Regulatory hurdles, quality control standards, IPR enforcement, and dependence on foreign sources for Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs).
Government policies: 'Pharma Vision 2020', support for R&D, and promotion of medical tourism.
GS3.imageDescription: Various medicine bottles and capsules on a table.
GS3.trivia: India supplies over 50% of the global demand for various vaccines.

GS3.headline: Government Boosts Agri-Tech Startups with New Fund and Incubation Support
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched a new fund and incubation program to support agri-tech startups.
• Aims to promote innovation in agriculture, including precision farming, supply chain management, and sustainable practices.
• The initiative seeks to address challenges like climate change, soil degradation, and market access for farmers.
• Expected to drive modernization and efficiency in the agricultural sector, enhancing farmer incomes.
gsSubTopics: Major crops - cropping patterns in various parts of the country
e-technology in the aid of farmers
Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices
prelimsPoints: Agri-tech involves the use of technology in agriculture to improve efficiency, productivity, and sustainability.
Examples include IoT sensors for soil monitoring, drones for crop spraying, and AI for yield prediction.
Government schemes like the Agri-startup challenge and Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY) support agricultural innovation.
mainsPoints: Benefits: Increased farmer income, enhanced food security, efficient resource use, climate resilience, and reduced post-harvest losses.
Challenges: Digital literacy among farmers, capital investment requirements, infrastructure gaps, market access, and data privacy concerns.
Role of government: Policy support, funding mechanisms, R&D promotion, and creating an enabling ecosystem for agri-tech.
Impact on rural economy: Job creation, value addition in agricultural produce, and attracting youth to the agriculture sector.
GS3.imageDescription: A drone flying over a field for agricultural monitoring.
GS3.trivia: India is the second-largest producer of agricultural products globally.

sources: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
